coredata.dc:title: Synergistic potential of 0D/2D amine-functionalized carbon quantum dots/g-C3N4 composites in thin film nanocomposite membranes for nanofiltration

coredata.dc:description: Water pollution has emerged as a major global concern with industrial development, emphasizing the need to maintain water quality and reduce pollution. Thin film nanocomposite (TFN) membranes offer energy efficiency, eco-friendliness, and high separation performance for water purification. While 0D/2D nanofillers have been extensively studied in hydrogen evolution and photocatalysis, their potential in membrane applications remained underexplored. Additionally, the use of toxic chemicals poses a barrier to advancing green and sustainable membrane fabrication. In this study, conventional piperazine was partially substituted with greener α-cyclodextrin during interfacial polymerization. Next, an eco-friendly hydrothermal method was employed to synthesize 0D amine-functionalized carbon quantum dots (NCQDs) using sustainable materials such as citric acid and urea. Finally, 0D/2D NCQDs/g-C₃N₄ (CG) composites were incorporated into polyesteramide selectivity layer. The as-synthesized CG-PCT membrane demonstrated a synergistic effect with a 1.6-fold improvement in pure water permeability (PWP) with respect to pristine PCT membranes, alongside high rejection rates for Na2SO4, MgSO4, and MgCl2, driven by Donnan exclusion and size exclusion mechanisms. This study highlights the potential of 0D/2D nanofillers in TFN membranes for nanofiltration, advancing green and sustainable membrane fabrication methods.

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors would like to acknowledge the financial support provided by National Natural Science Foundation of China (grant number: 22108118), State Key Laboratory of Physical Chemistry of Solid Surfaces, Xiamen University (grant number: 2024X32), Xiamen University Malaysia Research Fund (grant number: XMUMRF/2024-C14/IENG/0073), and Hengyuan International Sdn. Bhd. (grant number: EENG/0003).

The PES substrate was prepared via phase inversion. The casting solution was prepared by dissolving PES and PEG in NMP with continuously stirring for 24 h at 60 \u00B0C. The amounts of PES and PEG were fixed at 18 wt% and 30 wt%, respectively. After degassing overnight, the membrane was casted using a casting blade on top of a flat glass support and immersed in DI water for solvent exchange. The PES substrate was then stored in DI water ensure complete phase inversion before further modification while eliminating residual solvent.Fig. 7 shows surface morphologies and roughness of the membranes from SEM and AFM, respectively. The pristine PES support exhibited a dense surface (Fig. 7(a1)). After interfacial polymerization, all membranes displayed nodular structures (Fig. 7(b1) to 7(f1)), along with a selective layer (Fig. 7(b2) to 7(f2)) [74]. Compared to the PT membrane, which was fabricated from the PIP aqueous monomer and TMC organic monomer, the addition of the \u03B1-CD monomer in PIP resulted in a PCT membrane with a thinner polyesteramide surface. This can be ascribed to the hydroxyl groups in \u03B1-CD with lower reactivity as compared to the amine groups in PIP, which reduces the reaction rate during interfacial polymerization. The surface roughness of the membranes increased with following sequence: PCT (104.2 nm) < C-PCT (149.7 nm) < PT (164.0 nm) < G-PCT (206.1 nm) < CG-PCT (221.1 nm). Interestingly, the PT membrane displayed a rougher surface than the C-PCT and PCT membranes. This may be because of easier interfacial polymerization resulted from the small molecular weight of the PIP monomer, generating a rougher polyamide layer surface. In contrast, the surface roughness of NCQDs-incorporated C-PCT membrane was higher with respect to the PCT membrane. The cationic \u2013NH2 groups in the NCQDs create an electrostatic repulsive force against the cationic PIP [21,30]. Additionally, the rate of diffusion for PIP is further reduced by the steric hindrance of NCQDs [75,76], leading to a rougher surface in the C-PCT membrane. The G-PCT membrane showed an increased surface roughness, which aligns with the findings described by Ge et al. [66]. Incorporating gCN will introduce steric hindrance, restricting the rate of diffusion for PIP towards the interface between aqueous and organic monomers solutions, thus limiting the cross-linking reaction with TMC organic monomer. Amongst all the membranes, the CG-PCT membrane exhibited the roughest surface following the incorporation of CG fillers, suggesting a stronger steric hindrance from the NCQDs and gCN which reduces the rate of diffusion for PIP.Furthermore, the contact duration of the aqueous on the support will also affect the properties of selective layer of the as-synthesized membranes. Generally, the longer contact duration provides longer time for aqueous monomers to react with membrane surface. Fig. 9(b) depicts the effect of the duration of aqueous expose on the support layer on the PWP and Na2SO4 rejection. Membranes with increased exposure duration exhibited higher PWP and Na2SO4 rejection, with 30 min identified as the optimum duration. It is speculated that a longer duration allows aqueous monomers, especially \u03B1-CD with lower reactivity to penetrate the porous support, thereby enhancing the hydrophilicity on membrane surface. However, when the exposure duration was extended to 40 min, a reduction in PWP was observed, along with a slight increase in Na2SO4 rejection, indicating a trade-off effect. This suggest that a thicker selective layer was formed, hindering water permeates across the membrane.The authors would like to acknowledge the financial support provided by National Natural Science Foundation of China (grant number: 22108118), State Key Laboratory of Physical Chemistry of Solid Surfaces, Xiamen University (grant number: 2024X32), Xiamen University Malaysia Research Fund (grant number: XMUMRF/2024-C14/IENG/0073), and Hengyuan International Sdn. Bhd. (grant number: EENG/0003).

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Water pollution has emerged as a major global concern with industrial development, emphasizing the need to maintain water quality and reduce pollution. Thin film nanocomposite (TFN) membranes offer energy efficiency, eco-friendliness, and high separation performance for water purification. While 0D/2D nanofillers have been extensively studied in hydrogen evolution and photocatalysis, their potential in membrane applications remained underexplored. Additionally, the use of toxic chemicals poses a barrier to advancing green and sustainable membrane fabrication. In this study, conventional piperazine was partially substituted with greener α-cyclodextrin during interfacial polymerization. Next, an eco-friendly hydrothermal method was employed to synthesize 0D amine-functionalized carbon quantum dots (NCQDs) using sustainable materials such as citric acid and urea. Finally, 0D/2D NCQDs/g-C₃N₄ (CG) composites were incorporated into polyesteramide selectivity layer. The as-synthesized CG-PCT membrane demonstrated a synergistic effect with a 1.6-fold improvement in pure water permeability (PWP) with respect to pristine PCT membranes, alongside high rejection rates for Na2SO4, MgSO4, and MgCl2, driven by Donnan exclusion and size exclusion mechanisms. This study highlights the potential of 0D/2D nanofillers in TFN membranes for nanofiltration, advancing green and sustainable membrane fabrication methods.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Wastewater treatment, reuse, and potable water production are essential for building a sustainable modern society. To achieve this, water purification technologies that are efficient, low energy consumption, and cost-effective should are desirable to meet the growing water demand in society [1]. Membrane technology has gained significant attention in the wastewater treatment industry for separating various pollutants before discharge or further applications. This is primarily due to its advantages, including low chemical consumption, environmental friendliness, easy installation, simple operating, and high separation efficiency [2,3]. Nanofiltration (NF) membranes are pressure-driven membranes generally used for water purification or wastewater treatment, attributing to their outstanding treatment performance, combined with low operating costs and pressure requirement [4,5]. Nevertheless, NF membranes often limit by trade-off effects, where an improvement in either pure water permeability (PWP) or rejection tends to compromise the other. The membrane performance can be improved by employing various modifications strategies, such as post-treatment methods [6], polymer additives [7–13], functionalized polymer [14–16], and alternative solvents or monomers [17–19].

Among various approaches, integrating nanomaterials with membranes to form thin film nanocomposites (TFN) appears as a promising strategy for tailoring membrane properties to meet specific requirements and applications. As the separation performance of the TFN membranes is primarily determined by the properties of selective layer, diverse dimensional nanomaterials, including zero-dimensional (0D) [20–22], one-dimensional (1D) [23], and two-dimensional (2D) [24–26], have been developed to be introduced into TFN membranes. By incorporating nanomaterials in membrane technology, the physicochemical characteristics of the membranes surface could be enhanced while facilitating additional channels for transporting water molecules across the interfacial voids formed within the selective layer, thus improve the membrane separation performance [27]. For instance, the integration of membrane technology and 0D carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have gained great interest, mainly owing to their simple and cheap productions, ultra-small sizes, tunable surface properties, abundant functional groups, and good biocompatibility [20,28,29]. Yang et al. incorporated CQDs to synthesize TFN membranes with enhanced PWP from 3.6 to 9.7 LMH/bar and amino acids rejection of more than 90 % [20]. This improvement is owing to the hydrophilic CQDs creating rapid pathways for water and ion transport. To further enhance performance, Sun et al. studied the roles of different functionalized CQDs (e.g., sulfonic, carboxyl, and amino groups) in TFN membranes for enhanced separation performance [21]. Compared to sulfonic- and carboxyl-functionalized CQDs, incorporating amino-functionalized CQDs (NCQDs) in TFN membranes demonstrated slightly lower PWP but higher rejection due to the denser polyamide selective layer resulting from the amino functional groups. In another study, Abidin et al. synthesized NCQDs and carboxyl-functionalized CQDs using ethylenediamine and ascorbic acid as precursors of amino and carboxyl groups, respectively [30]. The NCQDs-incorporated TFN membranes resulted a more negatively charged compared to CQDs, providing higher electrostatic repulsion against salt anions (e.g., Cl− and SO4 2−), thereby enhancing rejection performance.

2D nanomaterials, such as covalent organic frameworks (COFs) [31,32], MXene [33,34], graphene oxide (GO) [35], metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) [36,37], and some emerging materials [38], have also been incorporated in TFN membranes, owing to their high specific surface area, ultrathin layers, large aspect ratios, and high lateral size. Graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4) is notable for low-cost, abundant material with high chemical and thermal stability, making it particularly promising for membrane separation applications [39–44]. Its layered structure is stabilized by van der Waals interactions, with tertiary amines linking the aromatic tri-s-triazine units in each layer. This structure allows smaller water molecules to pass through quickly via the nanochannels while rejecting larger salt particles through size exclusion effects and electrostatic repulsion [45,46]. For instance, Ye et al. incorporated g-C3N4 nanosheets into TFN membranes for desalination of dye-contaminated wastewater [47]. The results showed a relatively high PWP and dyes rejection of 28.4 LMH/bar and 99.3 %, respectively. Similarly, Chen et al. introduced g-C3N4 nanosheets into TFN membranes, resulting an increased PWP of 18.8 LMH/bar and Na2SO4 rejection of 84.0 % at trace loading amount of 0.0025 wt% [48]. These phenomena can be ascribed to the additional nanochannels created by the g-C3N4 nanosheets which facilitate water molecule transport.

Compared to single-type nanofillers, multi-dimensional composites offer unexpected synergistic effects and overcome the inherent limitations of each individual component, while retaining the unique functionality of each. A significant example of this is the agglomeration of 0D nanofillers during the synthesis of TFN membranes, which occurs due to their high surface energy. Wang et al. synthesized TFN membranes using 0D/2D TiO2@GO composites, resulting in PWP and Na2SO4 rejection of 5.61 LMH/bar and 98.8 %, respectively. The presence of GO mitigated the agglomeration of TiO2 nanoparticles by embedding them within the interlayer spaces of GO sheets [49].

Besides, the synthesis of TFN membranes often involves non-renewable materials, which may lead to secondary pollution or harm towards human health. For instance, the common aqueous monomers used are derived from non-sustainable resources such as piperazine, which is fabricated from crude-oil based materials. In response to the sustainable development goals (SDGs) promoted by United Nations, the sustainability of membrane technology can be achieved by implementing “Green Chemistry”, a concept developed by Anastas and Warner in 1998 [50]. Considering this, a greener alternative could be plant-based monomers cyclodextrin (CD), an inexpensive and commercially available frustoconical macrocyclic biomaterial derived from starch [51,52]. CD could be used as an aqueous monomer in forming the selective layer of TFN membranes for effective wastewater treatment [53,54]. This is due to its unique characteristics, including 3D hollow bowl structure with varying inner cavity sizes (e.g., α-, β-, and γ-CD), a hydrophilic outer wall, hydrophobic inner cavities, and abundant hydroxyl groups that enable crosslinking and modification [55]. In addition to the use of greener aqueous monomers, the green concept can also be applied in the synthesis of fillers for TFN membranes. Selecting more sustainable synthesis methods and raw materials can further improve the sustainability of TFN membranes. For instance, the hydrothermal method is considered a simpler and more environmentally benign process as it uses water as the solvent and requires only normal heating, without additional procedures or toxic solvents.

In this work, the principle of sustainable synthesis of TFN membrane is applied in both the fabrication of fillers and TFN membranes. Specifically, α-CD, a plant-based monomer is chosen as a greener alternative to replace piperazine in mixed aqueous monomer systems. α-CD with the smallest cavity size with respect to β- and γ-CD is preferred for fabricating membranes with better rejection performance. Additionally, NCQDs are synthesized via a greener hydrothermal method using urea and citric acid as the sustainable nitrogen and carbon sources, respectively. This work aims to combine the properties of 0D NCQDs and 2D g-C3N4 to form TFN membranes, promoting synergistic enhancements in PWP and rejection performance. It is worth noting that coupling g-C3N4 nanosheets with NCQDs has been studied in the context of the hydrogen evolution reaction and photocatalysis [56–61]. Nevertheless, the effects and roles of 0D/2D composites, specifically NCQDs/g-C3N4 as fillers in membranes, have not been explored in depth. In summary, hydrothermal method was employed to fabricate NCQDs/g-C3N4 fillers and integrated into the selective layer through one-step interfacial polymerization to form TFN membranes. Incorporating these 0D/2D nanocomposites, the separation performance of the membranes significantly enhances through synergistic effects. The NCQDs improve hydrophilicity and surface functionalization, facilitating rapid pathways for water molecule transport while maintaining strong electrostatic interactions for ion rejection. Simultaneously, g-C3N4 provides a layered structure conducive to nanochannel formation, enabling effective water transport and size exclusion mechanisms. Together, this dual-component approach optimizes membrane performance by enhancing permeability, salt rejection, and stability. This work aims to: (1) integrate the “Green Chemistry” concept in the synthesis of NCQDs/g-C3N4 fillers and TFN membranes; (2) optimize separation performance by adjusting parameters such as NaOH loading, aqueous monomers contact duration, interfacial polymerization duration, drying time, piperazine to α-CD (PIP:CD) ratio, and filler loading during interfacial polymerization; and (3) investigate the roles of 0D/2D composites, specifically NCQDs/g-C3N4 fillers, in enhancing membrane separation performance. These key findings are expected to offer valuable insights into integrating multi-dimensional composites with greener alternative to promote the sustainable fabrication while enhancing the separation performance of TFN membranes.

The chemicals were all utilized without additional purification. Piperazine (PIP, >99.5 %), 1,3,5-benzenetricarbonyl trichloride (TMC, >99 %), sodium chloride (NaCl, analytical reagent), magnesium chloride (MgCl2, analytical reagent), magnesium sulphate (MgSO4, analytical reagent), sodium sulphate (Na2SO4, analytical reagent), hexane (analytical reagent), α-CD (≥98 %), urea (99.0–100.5 %), citric acid (99 %), ethanol (analytical reagent), and dicyandiamide (99 %), ethylene glycol (EG) and polyethylene glycol (PEG) were purchased from Sigma Aldrich. N-methyl-2-pyrrolidone (NMP, analytical reagent) and polyethersulfone (PES) were acquired from Chemiz and Solvay S.A., respectively.

The PES substrate was prepared via phase inversion. The casting solution was prepared by dissolving PES and PEG in NMP with continuously stirring for 24 h at 60 °C. The amounts of PES and PEG were fixed at 18 wt% and 30 wt%, respectively. After degassing overnight, the membrane was casted using a casting blade on top of a flat glass support and immersed in DI water for solvent exchange. The PES substrate was then stored in DI water ensure complete phase inversion before further modification while eliminating residual solvent.

The g-C3N4 was synthesized using the calcination method [62]. First, the desired amount of dicyandiamide was measured and transferred into a crucible. The initial calcination was performed at 550 °C for 4 h with 3 °C/min. Using a pestle and mortar, the yellowish solids were then ground into fine powder, followed by a second calcination at 500 °C for 2 h with 5 °C/min to obtain the g-C3N4 powder. The g-C3N4 was denoted as “gCN”.

The NCQDs were fabricated via facile hydrothermal method [63]. First, a weight ratio of citric acid and urea at 1:2 were dissolved in DI water. The solution was then proceeded with hydrothermal treatment at 160 °C for 4 h. The prepared NCQDs solution was obtained after removing large particles by centrifugation.

The NCQDs/g-C3N4 fillers were fabricated via hydrothermal treatment [64], as illustrated in Fig. 1 . 0.25 g g-C3N4 was suspended in 40 mL ethanol and 3 mL NCQDs solution was then introduced with continuous stirring. The suspension was proceeded with hydrothermal treatment at 120 °C for 2 h. After cooling, the blue solids were washed using ethanol and dried at 60 °C to ensure complete elimination of solvents. The NCQDs/g-C3N4 fillers were denoted as “CG”.

The organic monomer solution was prepared by dissolving TMC (0.15 wt%) in hexane, while the aqueous monomer solution was prepared by mixing PIP, α-CD, and 1.0 g/L CG fillers in DI water with PIP to α-CD (PIP:CD) ratio of 1:1. The aqueous monomer solution was poured onto the PES substrate and was removed after 30 min. The membrane was soaked in the organic monomer solution to allow the interfacial polymerization for 2 min. The prepared membranes were dried for 5 min at atmospheric conditions. A control membrane without filler was synthesized for performance comparison. Fig. 2 illustrates the membrane synthesis process.

To optimize the performances of the membranes, various parameters were modified, including NaOH loading (e.g., 0.0, 1.0, and 2.0 g/L), PIP:CD ratios (e.g., 2.0:0.0, 1.75:0.25, 1.5:0.5, 1.0:1.0, 0.5:1.5, 0.25:1.75, and 0.0:2.0), contact durations of aqueous monomers (e.g., 2, 5, 10, 20, 30, and 40 min), duration of the interfacial polymerization (e.g., 1, 2, 5, and 10 min), drying condition (e.g., 25 and 60 °C), and loadings of CG fillers (e.g., 0.1, 0.2, 0.5, 1.0, and 2.0 g/L). The total PIP:CD ratios were fixed at 2 wt%. Table 1 lists the designation of the fillers and membranes for better understanding.

All samples were dried under vacuum at 30 °C for 24 h before characterization. X-ray diffraction (XRD, PANalytical) was conducted to study the crystalline structure of various fillers (e.g., gCN and CG) over a 2
θ
range of 5°–70° with a step size of 0.02°. Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR, Nicolet 8700 spectrometer, Thermo Scientific) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, K-Alpha, Thermo Scientific) were employed to analyze the chemical structure, surface functional groups, and chemical composition of the fillers and membranes (e.g., PES, PT, PCT, C-PCT, G-PCT, and CG-PCT). Surface morphologies of the fillers and membranes were characterized using transmission electron microscopy (TEM, JEM-2100Plus, JEOL) and scanning electron microscopy (SEM, S4800, Hitachi), respectively. Surface roughness of the membranes was examined via atomic force microscopy (AFM, Dimension® Icon, Bruker). Membrane hydrophilicity was assessed using water contact angle measurements with a goniometer (Drop Meter A100P, Maist). The zeta potential of the membranes was determined using the streaming potential method with an electro-kinetic analyzer (SurPASS3, Anton Paar) in 0.001 M KCl solution, adjusting the pH levels of the background solution from 3 to 11 at 25 °C.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Molecular weight cut-off (MWCO), mean pore size, and pore size distribution
ce:section.ce:para: The effective pore size of nanofiltration membranes, typically ranging from 0.5 to 2 nm, is difficult to measure directly using conventional instrumentation. Hence, a solute separation technique was utilized to determine the molecular weight cut-off (MWCO), mean pore size, and pore size distribution of the synthesized membranes [65]. This method followed a similar procedure to salt rejection measurements, substituting salt solutions with electroneutral organic EG or PEG molecules of seven different molecular weights (e.g., 62, 200, 400, 600, 1000, 1500, and 2000 Da). The initial PEG feed concentration was prepared at 0.2 g/L and membranes were stabilized in each feed solution for 1 h under a pressure of 10 bar before conducting the rejection tests. PEG concentrations in the feed and permeate were measured using a total organic carbon analyzer (TOC-L, Shimadzu). The MWCO was then determined from the rejection curve and defined as the molecular weight at which the membrane achieved at 90 % rejection of PEG.

The Stoke radius (
r s
, nm) of PEG at different molecular weights was calculated using the following equation [65]:
(1)

represents different molecular weights of PEG (Da).

Based on the equation, the Stokes radii of EG 62, PEG 200, PEG 400, PEG 600, PEG 1000, PEG 1500, and PEG 2000 were approximately 0.1667 nm, 0.3200 nm, 0.4708 nm, 0.5901 nm, 0.7843 nm, 0.9831 nm, and 1.1539 nm, respectively.

Assuming minimal hydrodynamic and steric interactions between the solutes and membrane pores, the pore size distribution of PCT and CG-PCT membranes can be described using the following probability density function [65]:

A lab-scale cross-flow filtration cell was utilized to assess the separation performance of the membranes at 25 °C and 10 bar. Inert nitrogen gas was introduced as pressure source. Before testing, all membranes samples were stabilized at 10 bar for 1 h. The measurements of PWP were taken and the salt rejection was studied using 1.0 g/L of four common inorganic salt solutions (e.g., Na2SO4, MgSO4, MgCl2, and NaCl). The PWP (LMH/bar) and the rejection (

To improve the potentials to be commercialized or implemented in industrial applications, a membrane should be able to maintain high performance under long-term operations. To evaluate the stability of PCT and CG-PCT membranes, a continuous filtration experiment using DI water and 1.0 g/L Na2SO4 solution. The PCT and CG-PCT membranes would first study the stability of membranes under continuous flow of DI water for 12 h, followed by using Na2SO4 solution for another 12 h at 10 bar. The data were collected every hour and calculated using Eqns. (3) and (4). The data recorded at the 1st hour for PWP and salt permeability was denoted as

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characteristics of gCN and CG fillers
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 3 shows the surface morphologies of gCN, NCQDs, and CG fillers analyzed using TEM. The pristine gCN nanosheets (Fig. 3(a1) and 3(a2)) exhibit a smooth 2D lamellar structure with uniformly distributed nanopores, indicated by the white and gray dots [64,66]. In contrast, the NCQDs are quasi-spherical with particle diameter of less than 10 nm and interplanar spacing of 0.25 nm, as shown in Fig. 3(b1) and 3(b2). Additionally, the presence of CG fillers and NCQDs is suggested by the white and gray dots, as well as the quasi-spherical structure in Fig. 3(c2).

Fig. 4 (a) displays the FTIR spectra of gCN and CG fillers, while Fig. 4(b) presents the enlarged spectra ranging 1000-2000 cm−1. The gCN exhibited characteristic g-C3N4 vibrations, including the stretching and bending of the triazine ring (e.g., 804 and 884 cm−1), C–N and CN heterocycles (e.g., 1000-1800 cm−1), and O–H and N–H groups (e.g., 3000-3500 cm−1) [67]. The CG fillers represent NCQDs/g-C3N4 fillers. In the FTIR spectra of CG fillers, peak intensity originating from gCN, such as triazine ring (e.g., 804 and 884 cm−1) and CN (e.g., 1455 and 1629 cm−1) were decreased, while C–N peaks (e.g., 1230, 1316, and 1399 cm−1) were shifted to 1237, 1334, and 1402 cm−1, respectively, as displayed in Fig. 4(b) [62,67]. Moreover, the broad peak at 3000-3500 cm−1 in gCN further broadened to 2700-3500 cm−1 in CG fillers, ascribed to O–H and N–H groups of pristine NCQDs, which displayed similar broad peaks from 2700 to 3800 cm−1 [67,68]. Additionally, new peaks at 1185, 1205, 1606, 1699, and 2896 cm−1 appeared in CG fillers, associated with C–NH–C, C–O–C, CN from the condensation of carboxylic acid and primary amine, CO or COOH from NCQDs edges, and aromatic C–H from NCQDs, respectively [21,67,68].

Fig. 4(c) demonstrates the XPS spectra of gCN and CG fillers, displaying characteristic peaks for C (1s), N (1s), and O (1s) at 285.08, 400.08, and 532.08 eV, respectively. The O (1s) intensity in CG fillers increases, while the N (1s) intensity decreases following the incorporation of NCQDs. Table 2 further details the concentrations of each component in gCN and CG fillers from XPS analysis. There is a significant increase in the O/N ratio of CG fillers up to 1.56 from the initial gCN of 0.06. This rise may be owing to the oxygen-containing and undoped groups (e.g., hydroxyl, carbonyl, amide, and carboxyl groups) in NCQDs, as illustrated in Fig. 1(b). The changes of peak intensity suggest the successful synthesis of CG fillers.

The crystalline structure of gCN and CG fillers was verified by XRD and the spectra are shown in Fig. 4(d). Both gCN and CG fillers exhibited two diffraction peaks at approximate 12.9° (e.g., (100) plane) and 27.5° (e.g., (002) plane), indicating the in-plane repeated tri-s-triazine units and interplanar stacking of conjugated aromatic structures characteristic of g-C3N4, respectively [62]. No additional peaks were observed in the XRD pattern of gCN, confirming the phase purity of gCN fillers. In contrast, the XRD pattern of CG fillers revealed two extra peaks at 19.3° and 22.9°, representing the (002) planes of the graphitic structure of NCQDs. These peaks are attributed to the presence of oxygen-containing and N–H functional groups, as well as the highly disordered structure of carbon [30,68].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characteristics and surface morphology of TFN membranes
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 5 (a) shows the full FTIR spectra while Fig. 5(b) displays the enlarged FTIR spectra from 800 to 2000 cm−1 of the TFN membranes. Additional peaks were observed for PT, PCT, C-PCT, G-PCT, and CG-PCT membranes at 1621 and 1638 cm−1, with respect to PES substrate. These peaks indicate the stretching vibration of amide CO and C–N, respectively, suggesting the presence of polyamide selective layer on the PES substrate [21,69]. However, a reduction in these peaks was observed in the PCT membrane with respect to the PT membrane, suggesting a decrease in amide compounds owing to the formation of polyesteramide layer [70]. In addition, all membranes exhibited a new broad peak ranging from 3200 to 3600 cm−1 with respect to the pristine PES substrate, attributed to the stretching vibrations of O–H from polyamide or polyesteramide layers in the membranes [21,69,71]. Nevertheless, the characteristic peaks of CG fillers were not appeared in the CG-PCT membrane, which may be due to the limited loading of CG fillers in the selective layer and the possibility of being masked by the polyesteramide layer. For instance, the peaks of 1100-1600 cm−1 as highlighted in Fig. 5(a), corresponded to heterocyclic C–N and CN, while the broad peak at 3200-3600 cm−1 represented N–H and O–H groups from gCN [66,67,72] and NCQDs [21,67]. Additionally, the peaks of 1200 and 1368 cm−1 were associated with C–O–C and –COOH from NCQDs [67], respectively. Since the FTIR results were unable to determine the effective incorporation of CG fillers in the CG-PCT membranes, the surface chemical compositions were further analyzed using XPS.

Fig. 6 illustrates the XPS spectra of PT, PCT, G-PCT, and CG-PCT membranes, while Table 3 presents the respective atomic concentrations of C (1s), N (1s), and O (1s) for the membranes. The N (1s) intensity in the G-PCT and CG-PCT membranes was significantly higher than the other membranes owing to the presence of additional nitrogen elements after the incorporation of gCN and CG fillers, respectively. Further quantitative analysis in Table 3 reveals the atomic concentrations of each element across the membranes, clearly showing elements changes while enabling the assessment of the degree of cross-linking for each membrane. Lau et al. [69] and Sun et al. [21] analyzed the degree of cross-linking using O/N and C/N ratios, respectively, where a higher O/N or C/N ratio indicates a lower cross-linking degree and vice versa. According to Table 3, the PCT membrane exhibits higher O/N and C/N ratios than the PT membrane, suggesting lower cross-linking degree of looser polyesteramide layer in the PCT membrane than the denser polyamide layer in the PT membrane. This finding aligns with previous research on the cross-linking degrees of polyamide and polyesteramide layers in thin-film composite (TFC) membranes [69]. After incorporating gCN fillers, the nitrogen and carbon contents show a notable increase in G-PCT membrane, yet a reduction in oxygen element owing to the absence of oxygen in the g-CN fillers and their abundant carbon and nitrogen elements. For the CG-PCT membrane, the oxygen element increased while the nitrogen element decreased when compared to the G-PCT membrane. This is likely ascribed to the various oxygen-containing elements in the NCQDs from the CG fillers. Additionally, when comparing the PT and PCT membranes, both G-PCT and CG-PCT membranes exhibited much lower O/N and C/N ratios, suggesting a higher cross-linking degree of these membranes. The formation of a denser polyesteramide layer in the G-PCT and CG-PCT membranes may be due to the amidation reaction between the gCN and CG fillers with TMC [73]. The XPS analysis further confirms the successful formation of a more loosely structured polyesteramide layer, achieved by partially substituting PIP using greener and more hydrophilic α-CD, and the incorporation of CG fillers into the polyesteramide layer.

Fig. 7 shows surface morphologies and roughness of the membranes from SEM and AFM, respectively. The pristine PES support exhibited a dense surface (Fig. 7(a1)). After interfacial polymerization, all membranes displayed nodular structures (Fig. 7(b1) to 7(f1)), along with a selective layer (Fig. 7(b2) to 7(f2)) [74]. Compared to the PT membrane, which was fabricated from the PIP aqueous monomer and TMC organic monomer, the addition of the α-CD monomer in PIP resulted in a PCT membrane with a thinner polyesteramide surface. This can be ascribed to the hydroxyl groups in α-CD with lower reactivity as compared to the amine groups in PIP, which reduces the reaction rate during interfacial polymerization. The surface roughness of the membranes increased with following sequence: PCT (104.2 nm) < C-PCT (149.7 nm) < PT (164.0 nm) < G-PCT (206.1 nm) < CG-PCT (221.1 nm). Interestingly, the PT membrane displayed a rougher surface than the C-PCT and PCT membranes. This may be because of easier interfacial polymerization resulted from the small molecular weight of the PIP monomer, generating a rougher polyamide layer surface. In contrast, the surface roughness of NCQDs-incorporated C-PCT membrane was higher with respect to the PCT membrane. The cationic –NH2 groups in the NCQDs create an electrostatic repulsive force against the cationic PIP [21,30]. Additionally, the rate of diffusion for PIP is further reduced by the steric hindrance of NCQDs [75,76], leading to a rougher surface in the C-PCT membrane. The G-PCT membrane showed an increased surface roughness, which aligns with the findings described by Ge et al. [66]. Incorporating gCN will introduce steric hindrance, restricting the rate of diffusion for PIP towards the interface between aqueous and organic monomers solutions, thus limiting the cross-linking reaction with TMC organic monomer. Amongst all the membranes, the CG-PCT membrane exhibited the roughest surface following the incorporation of CG fillers, suggesting a stronger steric hindrance from the NCQDs and gCN which reduces the rate of diffusion for PIP.

The hydrophilicity of the as-synthesized membranes is analyzed by measuring water contact angles as demonstrated in Fig. 8 (a). The water contact angle of the membranes decreased in the following sequence: PT (32.7°) > C-PCT (32.2°) > PCT (27.1°) > G-PCT or CG-PCT (26.7°). The PT membrane exhibited the highest water contact angle at 32.7°. When PIP was partially substituted with α-CD, the hydrophilicity of PCT membranes increased with significant reduction of water contact angle to 27.1° with respect to PT membrane, ascribed to the hydrophilic properties of α-CD, such as hydrophilic hydroxyl groups and unique internal hydrophobic hollow cavity structure, which effectively improve membrane hydrophilicity [52,71,77]. However, the C-PCT membrane exhibited lower hydrophilicity than the PCT membrane due to higher water contact angle. This may be attributed to the –NH2 groups in NCQDs being less hydrophilic than the –OH groups in α-CD, as the –OH groups have a stronger affinity towards water molecules to form hydrogen bonds, owing to the higher electronegativity of oxygen compared to nitrogen. In contrast, the incorporation of hydrophilic gCN fillers further reduced the water contact angle of the G-PCT membrane as compared to PCT membrane, facilitating hydrogen bonding with water molecules, thereby enhanced the affinity of membrane surface towards water [78]. Notably, similar water contact angle was observed for the G-PCT and CG-PCT membranes, suggesting that the low loading of NCQDs in CG fillers had a negligible effect on membrane hydrophilicity.

Fig. 8(b) depicts the surface charge of as-synthesized membranes evaluated via zeta potential measurements from pH 3 to 10. All TFN membranes showed decreasing trend as pH values increased owing to more negatively-charged surface of the membranes originated from the deprotonation of –COOH groups and adsorption of –OH groups [69]. At pH 7, the PT membrane with pure polyamide layer exhibited negatively-charged surface. When α-CD was added as mixed monomers to form polyesteramide layer, the zeta potential of PCT membranes significantly reduced, indicating more negative of the membrane surface. This may be because of the lower reactivity hydroxyl groups from α-CD compared to that of the secondary amines from PIP when undergo interfacial polymerization process, converting more unreacted acyl chloride groups into carboxyl groups via hydrolysis [79]. For the C-PCT membrane, the zeta potential at pH 7 was more negative than as observed for the PT membrane, ascribing to the amino groups in NCQDs, which introduce greater negativity to the membrane [80]. However, it was less negative compared to the PCT membrane, possibly because the nitrogen-containing groups from the NCQDs may partially neutralize the negative charge contributed by the hydroxyl groups in α-CD. After the addition of gCN, the G-PCT membrane showed a notable reduction in zeta potential, exhibiting more negative membrane surface as compared to PCT membrane at pH 7. This might be owing to nitrogen-rich structure of gCN with high density of amine and imine groups that deprotonate at higher pH levels, thus enhanced the surface charge. This phenomenon is aligned with the finding previously reported by Ge et al., the gCN with average zeta potential of -3.56 mV will also lead to an increase in negative surface potential of the membranes [66]. Comparing with G-PCT membrane, the CG-PCT membrane exhibited much less negative zeta potential, resulting from the synergistic effect where the negative charge from gCN was moderated by the presence of NCQDs. Although gCN possesses high surface charge density, the presence of NCQDs in CG fillers introduced a variety of functional groups, which might mitigate the overall charge density.

Within the scope of this study, various synthesis parameters such as NaOH loadings, aqueous components, contact duration of the aqueous phase, duration of interfacial polymerization, drying temperature, and ratio of PIP:CD were optimized to enhance the performance of as-synthesized membranes. NaOH functions as a pH adjuster, creating an alkaline environment that enhances the reactivity of aqueous monomers (e.g., PIP and α-CD) with acyl chlorides in the organic phase, leading to a faster and more efficient formation of the polyesteramide layer [69]. The effect of NaOH loading in aqueous monomers solution towards the PWP and Na2SO4 rejection of the membranes is shown in Fig. 9 (a). As the NaOH concentration increases during interfacial polymerization, the PWP of the membranes decreases, while the Na2SO4 rejection shows an initial decline followed by an increase. However, excessive NaOH may over-crosslinking or create defects on the selective layer, which can adversely affect membrane performance. It can be seen that the membrane without NaOH during interfacial polymerization exhibited the highest PWP and Na2SO4 rejection. This may be attributed to a lower interfacial polymerization degree, leading to a lower degree cross-linking and higher PWP. Thus, the following membranes were fabricated using an aqueous monomers solution without NaOH during interfacial polymerization.

Furthermore, the contact duration of the aqueous on the support will also affect the properties of selective layer of the as-synthesized membranes. Generally, the longer contact duration provides longer time for aqueous monomers to react with membrane surface. Fig. 9(b) depicts the effect of the duration of aqueous expose on the support layer on the PWP and Na2SO4 rejection. Membranes with increased exposure duration exhibited higher PWP and Na2SO4 rejection, with 30 min identified as the optimum duration. It is speculated that a longer duration allows aqueous monomers, especially α-CD with lower reactivity to penetrate the porous support, thereby enhancing the hydrophilicity on membrane surface. However, when the exposure duration was extended to 40 min, a reduction in PWP was observed, along with a slight increase in Na2SO4 rejection, indicating a trade-off effect. This suggest that a thicker selective layer was formed, hindering water permeates across the membrane.

The duration of interfacial polymerization between organic and aqueous monomers affects the formation of selective layer. An optimum duration of interfacial polymerization is required to achieve a defect-free and uniform selective layer. By referring to Fig. 9(c), with increasing interfacial polymerization duration, the PWP decreased from 1 min to 10 min owing to the denser and thicker selective layer formed on the membrane, thus hindering water molecule passage through the membranes. The membrane showed a lower Na2SO4 rejection at 1 min may be ascribed to insufficient interfacial polymerization time, leading to the formation of a thinner and defective selective layer. The optimum duration was found to be 2 min which provided the highest salt rejection and a comparable PWP. However, the PWP and Na2SO4 rejection of the membrane gradually decreased as the polymerization duration increased from 2 to 10 min. Prolonged polymerization may lead to: (1) the formation of thicker and denser selective layer, increasing mass transfer resistance and thereby compromising the PWP of the membrane; and (2) uneven polymer growth, where defects or inconsistencies, such as microvoids or unreacted regions, may form in the membrane, resulting in reduced Na2SO4 rejection.

Solvents used in interfacial polymerization evaporate during drying, solidifying the selective layer. An optimum drying temperature ensures uniformity and prevents defects such as cracks or voids that can compromise the selectivity and permeability of a membrane. The drying conditions including temperature and time, must be carefully controlled to optimize the structural integrity and performance of a membrane. In this study, with the drying duration fixed at 5 min, the membranes were dried under two different conditions (e.g., room temperature and 60 °C) as shown in Fig. 9(d). As observed, the membranes dried at room temperature possessed both enhanced PWP and salt rejection. Lower drying temperature may result in a slower and more controlled solvent evaporation, which promotes uniform layer formation with fewer defects, leading to enhanced performance. In short, the optimum conditions for selective layer formation were determined to be 30 min of aqueous monomers expose on the support layer, 2 min of interfacial polymerization after adding the organic monomer, and drying at room temperature for 5 min.

During interfacial polymerization, the monomers such as PIP and α-CD with different reactivity will affect the structure of the selective layer. Fig. 9(e) shows the influence of the ratio of mixed monomers such as PIP and α-CD used towards the PWP and Na2SO4 rejection. The membranes prepared from PIP only (e.g., 2.0:0.0) exhibited the highest Na2SO4 rejection with the lowest PWP, while the membranes fabricated with α-CD only (e.g., 0.0:2.0) had extremely high PWP but the lowest Na2SO4 rejection. As the α-CD ratio increased, the PWP of the membranes gradually increased, while Na2SO4 rejection decreased. This may be because of (1) the decreased thickness of the selective layer caused by the presence of α-CD which alleviates the mass transfer resistance; (2) the unique hollow cavity structure in α-CD that provides additional water transport pathways; and (3) the looser polyesteramide layer formed during the interfacial polymerization that results in the membranes with an enlarged pore size and lower degree of cross-linking, as evidenced by the C/N ratio of G-PCT characterized using XPS (Table 3) [70]. The optimum PIP:CD ratio was found to be 1.0:1.0, providing the highest Na2SO4 rejection without compromising the PWP.

Besides, the amount of CG fillers incorporated during in the TFN membranes plays a vital role in membrane performance without causing agglomeration issue. Fig. 9(f) illustrates the impact of filler loading on the PWP and Na2SO4 rejection. The PWP of membranes slightly improved with increasing CG filler loading from 0.1 to 0.5 g/L, owing to the enhanced hydrophilicity of the membrane after introducing CG fillers that consist abundant hydrophilic functional groups, along with the partial substitution of PIP with α-CD as mixed aqueous monomers. This observation is aligned with the results obtained in water contact angle in Fig. 8(a). In contrast, the Na2SO4 rejection of the membrane decreased as the CG filler loading increased from 0.1 to 0.5 g/L, possibly due to the formation of larger or more irregular pores with higher filler loading. The membrane with 1.0 g/L CG fillers possessed the optimum separation performance, with a PWP and Na2SO4 rejection of 6.08 LMH/bar and 96.4 %, respectively. Further increasing the filler loading to 2.0 g/L resulted in diminished separation performance, likely due to filler agglomeration.

To compare the MWCO of PCT and CG-PCT membranes with the addition of CG fillers, the membrane pore size was analyzed through rejection experiments using neutral organic PEG molecules of varying molecular weights. Fig. 10 (a) depicts the PEG rejection performance for molecular weights ranging from 62 to 2000 Da, while Fig. 10(b) shows the pore size distribution calculated using probability density function. Both membranes exhibited high retention (>90 %) for PEG molecules above 400 Da. From Fig. 10(a), the MWCO of the PCT was 276 Da, compared to 364 Da for the CG-PCT membrane. The increase in MWCO for the CG-PCT membrane is attributed to the presence of CG fillers, where gCN nanosheets induce a looser structure by creating interfacial defects within the polyesteramide layer [81]. The corresponding Stokes radii of PEG molecules were 0.2363 and 0.2479 nm, respectively, as summarized in Table 4 . Fig. 10(b) shows that the pore diameters of both membranes were primarily distributed below 1.0 nm. The CG-PCT membrane exhibited an average pore size of 0.42 nm, slightly smaller than the 0.43 nm observed for the PCT membrane. However, the incorporation of CG fillers resulted in a broader pore size distribution for the CG-PCT membrane. This aligns with the salts rejection performance, where larger pores contributed to a slight reduction in the rejection of larger salts (e.g., Na2SO4 and MgSO4), while smaller pores enhanced rejection for smaller salts (e.g., MgCl2).

Fig. 11 (a) depicts the Na2SO4, MgSO4, MgCl2, and NaCl rejections for the PCT, C-PCT, G-PCT, and CG-PCT membranes. Compared to the PCT membrane, the C-PCT and G-PCT membranes showed an increase in PWP with a slight reduction in salt rejections. Among all, the CG-PCT membrane after the incorporation of CG fillers exhibited the best overall performances with a PWP of 6.08 LMH/bar and Na2SO4, MgSO4, MgCl2, and NaCl rejections of 97.4 %, 96.9 %, 66.7 %, and 10.9 %, respectively. This was due to the formation of looser polyesteramide layer, unique hollow structure of α-CD and the abundant hydrophilic functional groups of CG fillers. Additionally, the presence of hydrophilic NCQDs in the CG-PCT membrane reduces water transfer resistance and generates additional water channels, which facilitates the movement of water molecules across the membrane matrix [21,82]. Furthermore, the hydrogen bonds formed between water molecules and negatively charged polyesteramide layer enhances the PWP of the membrane [83]. The incorporation of gCN into membranes not only creates additional voids in the selective layer but also introduces hydrophilic amino groups that promote water molecule penetration into the interlayer, leading to an overall improvement in PWP [66]. The integration of NCQDs and g-C3N4 creates a composite with increased surface roughness and additional voids, providing multiple water transport pathways and enhancing overall permeability.

The rejection performance of CG-PCT membrane adhered to the sequence of Na2SO4 > MgSO4 > MgCl2 > NaCl. This sequence aligns with Donnan exclusion theory which states the membrane surface exerts stronger electrostatic repulsion towards divalent anions (e.g., SO4 2−) with respects to monovalent anions (e.g., Cl−) [84]. By comparing Na2SO4 and MgSO4, both containing similar divalent SO4 2− ions, the negatively charged CG-PCT membrane exhibited higher Na2SO4 rejection, ascribed to stronger electrostatic attraction exerted by the negatively charged membrane surface towards Mg2+ cations compared to Na+ cations. For MgCl2 and NaCl which both consist of monovalent Cl− ions, MgCl2 with larger hydration radius of Mg2+ exhibited higher rejection, in accordance with the principle of size exclusion [85]. Besides, the CG-PCT membrane showed a higher MgCl2 rejection but a lower NaCl rejection with respect to PCT membrane. This could be explained by the reduced negatively charged surface of the CG-PCT membrane, leading to weaker electrostatic attraction towards Mg2+ and weaker electrostatic repulsion against Cl−. Furthermore, the incorporation of CG fillers may reduce the membrane pore size, as shown in Fig. 10, enhancing the size exclusion effect for MgCl2 which has a larger size.

A membrane that able to operate over a long duration with stable and excellent separation performance is one of the crucial factors influencing its potential for commercialization in real-world water treatment applications. Fig. 11(b) and 11(c) illustrate the results of stability tests conducted for PCT and CG-PCT membranes using a 1 g/L Na2SO4 solution under 10 bar for 12 h. Both membranes exhibited stable PWP with slight reduction in Na2SO4 rejection. Specifically, the PCT membrane showed a decrease in rejection from 98.5 % to 95.8 %, while the CG-PCT membrane experienced a reduction from 96.2 % to 92.0 %. These results confirm that both membranes exhibit good stability over prolonged operation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Performance comparison with commercial membranes and literature
ce:section.ce:para: Table 5 compares the three commercial membranes (e.g., NF90, NF270, and BW30XLE), other reported NF membranes as well as the as-prepared PCT and CG-PCT membranes. Among all TFC membranes, the PCT membrane exhibited the lowest PWP with improved salt rejections. Notably, the CG-PCT membrane after the incorporation of CG fillers showed a higher PWP than the NCQDs TFN membranes, along with improved salt rejections. Furthermore, the performance of CG-PCT membranes was comparable with other state-of-the-art of TFN membranes and remained stable under 10 bar with excellent salts rejection performance.

In conclusion, the 0D/2D CG fillers were prepared via a greener hydrothermal method involving NCQDs and gCN. The TFN membrane were fabricated through a facile one-step interfacial polymerization between CG fillers, α-CD, and PIP, with TMC on a PES substrate. In this study, the concept of sustainability was incorporated by partially substituting PIP with α-CD, which is derived from starch, and employing a more environmentally benign hydrothermal method along with greener raw materials, such as DI water, ethanol, urea, and citric acid in the synthesis of CG fillers. The FTIR and XPS results indicated the successful synthesis of CG fillers, formation of polyesteramide layer on PES substrate, and incorporation of CG fillers to form TFN membranes. Upon optimization, CG-PCT membrane exhibited improvement in overall membrane separation performance as compared to PCT membrane, whereby the PWP has increased to 6.1 LMH/bar and comparable various salts rejection with MgCl2 rejection enhanced from 49.6 to 66.7 %, assigning to the partial substitution of PIP with α-CD and the incorporation of CG fillers. The roles and mechanisms of the CG-PCT membrane can be categorized into contributions from NCQDs, g-C3N4, and the polymer matrix: (i) NCQDs enhance hydrophilicity through their abundant functional groups, reducing the water contact angle and facilitating water molecule transport. The cationic amino groups in NCQDs increase the surface charge of the membrane, reducing electrostatic attraction to Mg2+ and electrostatic repulsion against Cl−, thereby improving rejection efficiency. (ii) g-C3N4 nanosheets introduce nanochannels that enable selective water molecule transport while excluding larger solutes via size exclusion. The hydrophilic nature and layered structure of g-C3N4 further contribute to improved water permeability and mechanical stability. The integration of NCQDs and g-C3N4 enhances surface roughness and creates additional voids, providing multiple water transport pathways and boosting overall permeability. (iii) α-cyclodextrin (α-CD), a greener alternative to conventional monomers like piperazine, significantly enhances membrane performance while promoting sustainability. Its abundant hydrophilic hydroxyl groups and unique hollow cavity improve membrane hydrophilicity and facilitate water transport. The lower reactivity of α-CD compared to piperazine results in a thinner, looser polyesteramide selective layer, effectively balancing high water permeability with effective salt rejection. Hence, the incorporation of multi-dimensional composites provides inspiration for synthesizing emerging TFN membranes with desirable separation performance using various composite materials synthesized through green methods or from sustainable raw materials.

Xie Yuen Gok: Writing – original draft, Validation, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Tian-Zhi Jia: Formal analysis. Xue-Li Cao: Formal analysis.
